paper_metadata.title: Fighting Climate Change: International Attitudes toward Climate Policies
paper_metadata.authors: Antoine Dechezleprêtre, Adrien Fabre, Tobias Kruse, Bluebery Planterose, Ana Sanchez Chico, Stefanie Stantcheva
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: 20 countries: Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Denmark, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Japan, Mexico, Poland, South Africa, South Korea, Spain, Turkey, Ukraine, United Kingdom, United States
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Large cross-country survey experiment on acceptability of carbon taxes with cash transfers/progressive transfers and a fossil-fuel tax, plus information treatments affecting support.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Believes the policy would reduce GHG emissions
determinants.definition: Belief that the tax will lower greenhouse-gas emissions.
determinants.mechanism: Expected abatement makes the tax seem worthwhile and increases support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: support for a given climate policy is strongly predicted by ... that the policy is helpful in reducing emissions

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Believes the policy would reduce air pollution
determinants.definition: Belief that the tax will lower air pollution.
determinants.mechanism: Local co-benefits increase the policy's perceived value and support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: support for a given climate policy is strongly predicted by ... that the policy is helpful in reducing emissions

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Believes the policy would have positive econ. effects
determinants.definition: Belief that the tax will benefit the economy or employment.
determinants.mechanism: Lower expected economic harm makes the tax more acceptable.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: Believing that a policy will positively impact the economy is associated with slightly higher policy support
determinants.notes: Weak predictor compared with emissions and self-interest.

determinants.definition: Belief that poorer households would be net losers from the tax.
determinants.mechanism: Expected regressivity reduces perceived fairness and support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: Respondents who believe that low-income earners will lose are less supportive of the policy

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Perceived private cost to own household
determinants.definition: Belief that the tax will financially hurt one's own household.
determinants.mechanism: Expected private costs lower willingness to support the tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: Those who think they will themselves lose from a given policy are much less likely to support it

determinants.definition: Belief that the tax design is fair.
determinants.mechanism: Fairness perceptions reduce opposition and raise acceptability.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers and related tax outcomes (Section IV.C; Figure 14).
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: there is a close connection between the respondent believing that a policy is fair and supporting it

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: cash transfers to the poorest households / cash transfers to constrained households
determinants.definition: Carbon-tax revenue is returned to poorer or constrained households.
determinants.mechanism: Targeted compensation makes the tax more progressive and easier to accept.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax revenue-use variants in Section III.A and carbon tax with progressive transfers in Figure 16.
determinants.evidence_quote: majority support for carbon taxes with transfers to the poorest or the most constrained households

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: fund environmental infrastructures, subsidize low-carbon technologies, or reduce income taxes
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Revenue recycling to green investment or income-tax cuts
determinants.definition: Carbon-tax revenue is earmarked for green investment, low-carbon subsidies, or personal income-tax cuts.
determinants.mechanism: Seen as effective and more acceptable than lump-sum rebates.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax revenue-use variants in Section III.A; Figure 10.
determinants.evidence_quote: Carbon taxes with revenues used to fund environmental infrastructures, subsidize low-carbon technologies, or reduce income taxes benefit from around 70 percent higher support
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: About 60% support in high-income countries; 75% in middle-income countries.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: equal cash transfers to all households
determinants.definition: Carbon-tax revenue is returned equally to all households.
determinants.mechanism: Less progressive than targeted or green recycling, lowering support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax with equal cash transfers in Section III.A; Figure 10.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with equal cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: Carbon taxes with revenues used to fund equal transfers to everyone only generate 37 percent support in high-income countries
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: 37% support in high-income countries; 59% in middle-income countries.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Revenue recycling to corporate tax cuts
determinants.definition: Carbon-tax revenue is used to cut corporate income taxes.
determinants.mechanism: Perceived as less progressive and less acceptable than green or targeted recycling.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax revenue-use variants in Section III.A; Figure 10.
determinants.evidence_quote: Carbon taxes used to reduce corporate taxes generate similarly low support as carbon taxes with equal transfers
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Support is similarly low as equal transfers and fossil-fuel taxes.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Car-dependent respondents are less supportive of taxes on fossil fuels and carbon taxes with cash transfers
determinants.definition: Reliance on cars for transport and fuel-intensive lifestyles.
determinants.mechanism: Higher expected fuel-tax burden lowers support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Taxes on fossil fuels and carbon taxes with cash transfers (Supplemental Appendix Figure A8, discussed in text).
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for fossil-fuel taxes and carbon taxes with cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: Car-dependent respondents are less supportive of taxes on fossil fuels and carbon taxes with cash transfers
determinants.page_or_section: Section III.C; Supplemental Appendix Figure A8
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Negative in Australia, France, Japan, Poland, and the United Kingdom.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Information video explaining how climate policies work
determinants.definition: Video explaining policy mechanisms, costs, and distributional implications.
determinants.mechanism: Clarifies tax incidence and effectiveness, boosting carbon-tax support.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: The Climate policies treatment ... significantly increase support
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: About 27% of the control-group mean; significant in all countries except Mexico.

determinants.definition: Exposure to both the climate-impacts and climate-policies videos.
determinants.mechanism: Combines concern about climate harms with explanation of policy design.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: The combination of the Climate impacts and Climate policies treatments have even stronger effects
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: About 33% of the control-group mean; strongest effects.

determinants.mechanism: Raises concern and knowledge more than tax support.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: The Climate impacts treatment has the smallest effects on support
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Did not substantially change public-policy support.
determinants.notes: Stronger for private behaviors than for tax support.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Believes the high-income earners will lose
determinants.definition: Belief that richer households would be net losers from the tax.
determinants.mechanism: Signals progressivity; association with support varies by country.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Support for carbon tax variants; Section IV.C and Supplemental Appendix Tables A13–A14.
determinants.outcome_variable: Support for carbon tax with cash transfers
determinants.evidence_quote: in a few countries ... the belief that the high-income earners will lose is even positively associated with support
determinants.page_or_section: Section IV.C; Supplemental Appendix Tables A13–A14
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Positive in Canada, France, India, Japan, Mexico, Spain, Turkey, and Ukraine.
